Details:
Membrane filtration, including ultrafiltration and microfiltration, varied in effectiveness for removal of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in full-scale studies [1756, 2175, 2441]. These studies observed 2 to 56 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), -30 to 43 percent removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), and similarly inconsistent removal of other PFASs including: perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS), perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA), perfluoroundecanoic acid (PFUnA), perfluorododecanoic acid (PFDoA), perfluorooctanesulfonamide (PFOSA), fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2), 2-(N-Ethyl-perfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-EtFOSAA), and 2-(N-Methylperfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-MeFOSAA).
Removal of PFOS by ultrafiltration in a pilot-scale potable reuse facility was greater than 80 percent, starting from very low influent concentration (1.25 ng/L) [2659]. The same study showed only 5 percent removal of PFOA, 14 percent removal of PFHxS, 4 percent removal of PFDA, and concentrations of PFNA, PFHxA, PFHpA, PFPeA, and PFBS that increased between the feed and the filtrate (i.e., negative removal).
One bench-scale study [2524] observed moderate to high (69 to 84 percent) removal of perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA) from spiked lab water by ultrafiltration membranes. Another bench-scale study of ultrafiltration found an average 21 percent removal of PFOA and 22 percent removal of PFOS using a conventional membrane, increasing to an average of 50 percent and 52 percent respectively using a chemically modified membrane [2666]. A third bench-scale study [2832] found 32 percent removal of PFOS and 23 percent removal of PFOA by ultrafiltration.
